docs.title: Telepresence Labs
docs.text: A comprehensive collection of hands-on labs for learning and mastering Telepresence — the tool that bridges your local development environment with a remote Kubernetes cluster.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: These labs provide a progressive learning path from basic cluster connectivity to advanced debugging techniques. Each lab builds upon the previous one, taking you from Telepresence fundamentals to production-grade workflows.

docs.title: Lab Series
docs.text: Lab Title Description 001 The Cluster Bridge Connectivity & DNS — access cluster services from your local machine 002 The Inner Dev Loop Global Intercept — route all service traffic to your local code 003 The Ghost Developer Personal Intercepts — develop without affecting teammates 004 The Context Clone Env Vars & Secrets — run locally with remote configuration 005 The Deep Dive Volume Mounts & Debugging — mount remote files and live debug

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before starting these labs, ensure you have:

  • A Kubernetes cluster (k3s, EKS, GKE, Minikube, etc.)
  • kubectl configured to access the cluster
  • Telepresence CLI installed
  • Python 3.8+ (for sample applications)
  • A text editor or IDE (VS Code recommended)

docs.title: How to Use These Labs
docs.text:
1. Start with Lab 001: Establish connectivity to your cluster
  2. Follow the sequence: Each lab builds on the previous one
  3. Run the commands: Every lab includes copy-paste ready commands
  4. Experiment: Modify the examples to match your own services

docs.title: MkDocs Labs Documentation
docs.text:
• A comprehensive collection of hands-on labs for learning and mastering MkDocs documentation skills.
  • These labs provide practical exercises covering MkDocs fundamentals, advanced concepts, and real-world scenarios.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
• This documentation site contains a series of progressive labs designed to take you from MkDocs basics to advanced documentation techniques.
  • Each lab builds upon the previous one, ensuring a structured learning path.

docs.title: 🚀 Lab Series [Example]
docs.text: Lab Title Description 000 Setup Environment setup and prerequisites 001 Basic MkDocs Site Creating your first MkDocs site 002 MkDocs Configuration Understanding configuration files 003 MkDocs Theme Customizing themes and appearance 004 MkDocs Extra Features Adding extra features and social links 005 MkDocs Plugins Extending functionality with plugins 006 MkDocs Extensions Markdown extensions and enhancements 007 MkDocs Navigation Building navigation structures 008 MkDocs Deployment Deploying to GitHub Pages and other platforms 009 Advanced Customization Theme overrides and custom CSS/JS 010 MkDocs Best Practices Optimization and maintenance 011 Real-World Projects Building complete documentation sites

docs.title: 📁 Project Structure
docs.text:
📂 mkdocs                         # Root project directory
 ┣ 📄 README.md                   # Project documentation
 ┣ 📄 vercel.json                 # Vercel deployment configuration
 ┣ 📦 requirements.txt            # Python dependencies (in mkdocs/)
 ┣ 📝 mkdocs.yml                  # Main MkDocs configuration
 ┣ 📂 Labs                        # Documentation content directory
 ┃ ┣ 📄 index.md                  # This file - labs overview
 ┃ ┗ 📄 welcome.md                 # Welcome page
 ┣ 📂 mkdocs                      # Modular configuration files
 ┃ ┣ 📝 01-mkdocs-site.yml        # Basic site configuration
 ┃ ┣ 🎨 02-mkdocs-theme.yml       # Material theme settings
 ┃ ┣ ➕ 03-mkdocs-extra.yml       # Extra features and social links
 ┃ ┣ 🔌 04-mkdocs-plugins.yml     # Plugin configurations
 ┃ ┣ 🧩 05-mkdocs-extensions.yml  # Markdown extensions
 ┃ ┣ 📑 06-mkdocs-nav.yml         # Navigation structure
 ┃ ┣ 📋 mkdocs.yml.schema.json    # Configuration schema
 ┃ ┣ 📂 overrides                 # Theme customizations
 ┃ ┃ ┣ 🧩 home.html               # Custom homepage
 ┃ ┃ ┣ 📂 assets                  # Custom assets
 ┃ ┃ ┣ 📂 partials                # Custom partial templates
 ┃ ┃ ┗ 📂 stylesheets             # Custom styles
 ┃ ┗ 📂 scripts                   # Utility scripts
 ┃   ┣ 🛠️ build_nav.sh            # Navigation builder
 ┃   ┣ 🛠️ build-multiarch.sh      # Multi-architecture build
 ┃   ┣ 🛠️ init_site.sh            # Site initialization
 ┃   ┗ 🛠️ init_vercel.sh          # Vercel setup
 ┗ 📂 mkdocs-site                 # Built site (generated)
   ┣ 📄 index.html                # Main page
   ┣ 📄 404.html                  # Error page
   ┣ 📄 sitemap.xml               # Site map
   ┣ 📂 assets                    # Site assets
   ┣ 📂 css                       # Custom CSS
   ┣ 📂 js                        # Custom JavaScript
   ┣ 📂 print_page                # Print-friendly pages
   ┗ 📂 search                    # Search index

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before starting these labs, ensure you have:

  • A Python 3.8+ environment
  • MkDocs installed (uv pip install mkdocs)
  • Basic knowledge of Markdown
  • Git for version control
  • A text editor or IDE

docs.title: How to Use These Labs
docs.text:
1. Start with Lab 000: Set up your environment
  2. Follow the sequence: Each lab builds on the previous one
  3. Read the instructions: Each lab folder contains a README.md with detailed steps
  4. Practice hands-on: Don’t just read - build the sites and experiment
  5. Experiment: Modify the configurations to understand how they work

docs.title: Running the Labs
docs.text:
Most labs can be run locally:

  • Use mkdocs serve to preview your site
  • Use mkdocs build to generate static files
  • Deploy to GitHub Pages with mkdocs gh-deploy
  • Or use any static hosting service

docs.title: 📚 Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By completing these labs, you will learn:

  • MkDocs installation and configuration
  • Theme customization and styling
  • Plugin integration and usage
  • Markdown extensions and advanced features
  • Navigation and site structure
  • Deployment strategies and automation
  • Best practices for documentation sites

docs.title: 🤝 Contributing to Labs
docs.text:
Found an issue or want to improve a lab?

  1. Fork the mkdocs repository
  2. Create your feature branch
  3. Make your changes
  4. Test thoroughly
  5. Submit a pull request

docs.title: 📞 Support
docs.text:
Need help with the labs?

  • Check the MkDocs documentation
  • Join our Discord community
  • Open an issue in the GitHub repository

**Happy documenting with MkDocs!*

docs.title: Connectivity & DNS
docs.text:
Objective

Verify that your local machine can see the cluster without an Ingress.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Telepresence creates a transparent network bridge between your local machine and a remote Kubernetes cluster. Once connected, you can access cluster-internal services using their native Kubernetes DNS names — no port-forwarding, no Ingress, no NodePort hacks.

mermaid graph LR A[Local Machine] -->|telepresence connect| B[Cluster VPN] B --> C[Service: backend.default] B --> D[Service: redis.default] B --> E[Service: postgres.default]

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Requirement Details Telepresence CLI Installed on your local machine kubectl Configured to access your remote cluster Cluster A running Kubernetes cluster (k3s, EKS, GKE, etc.) Services At least one service deployed in the cluster

docs.title: Step 1: Install Telepresence CLI
docs.text:
macOSLinux 
brew install datawire/blackbird/telepresence-oss

sudo curl -fL https://app.getambassador.io/download/tel2oss/releases/download/v2.27.3/telepresence-linux-amd64 \
  -o /usr/local/bin/telepresence
sudo chmod +x /usr/local/bin/telepresence

Verify the installation:

telepresence version
Expected Output 
OSS Client : v2.27.3
Root Daemon: not running
User Daemon: not running

docs.title: Step 2: Connect to the Cluster
docs.text:
telepresence connect
Expected Output 
✔ Launched Daemon
✔ Connected to context default, namespace default

Kubeconfig

Make sure your KUBECONFIG environment variable points to a kubeconfig file with the correct server IP (not 127.0.0.1 unless the cluster is local). For remote clusters, update the server: field to the cluster’s LAN/public IP.

docs.title: Step 3: Verify the Connection
docs.text:
Check the Telepresence status:

telepresence status
Expected Output 
User Daemon: Running
Root Daemon: Running
  Version   : v2.27.3
  DNS       : ...
  Also Proxy: ...

docs.title: Task 1: Reach an Internal Service via K8s DNS
docs.text:
Use curl to access an internal service using its full Kubernetes DNS name:

curl http://nginx.default.svc.cluster.local

Or the short form (within the same namespace):

curl http://nginx.default

Expected Result

You should see the nginx welcome page HTML or whatever response the service returns — directly from your local terminal, without any Ingress or port-forward.

docs.title: Task 2: Access a Cluster-Internal Database
docs.text:
Deploy a Redis instance in your cluster (if not already running):

kubectl run redis --image=redis:latest --port=6379
kubectl expose pod redis --port=6379 --name=redis

Then connect to it directly from your local machine:

redis-cli -h redis.default
Expected Output 
redis.default:6379> PING
PONG

PostgreSQL Example

If you have a PostgreSQL service running in the cluster: 

psql -h postgres.default -U myuser -d mydb

docs.title: Task 3: Verify DNS Resolution
docs.text:
Confirm that your local machine resolves cluster DNS names:

nslookup nginx.default

Or:

dig nginx.default.svc.cluster.local

docs.title: Cleanup
docs.text:
When you’re done, disconnect from the cluster:

telepresence quit

docs.title: Outcome
docs.text:
What You Learned

  • Telepresence bridges your local network to the cluster’s SDN
  • You can access any cluster service using its Kubernetes DNS name
  • No Ingress, NodePort, or port-forwarding is required
  • Standard CLI tools (curl, redis-cli, psql) work transparently
  • Your local machine behaves as if it’s inside the cluster network

docs.title: Global Intercept
docs.text:
Objective

Route all traffic from a remote service to your local code — bypass the entire CI/CD pipeline for instant iteration.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
A Global Intercept captures all incoming traffic to a service in the cluster and redirects it to a process running on your local machine. This means you can edit code locally, and see the changes reflected immediately when the service is accessed — no Docker builds, no image pushes, no kubectl apply.

```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client as Frontend / Client participant Cluster as K8s Service (backend) participant TP as Telepresence Agent participant Local as Local Dev Machine

Client->>Cluster: HTTP Request
Cluster->>TP: Intercepted
TP->>Local: Forwarded to localhost:8080
Local-->>TP: Response from local code
TP-->>Cluster: Response
Cluster-->>Client: Response (from your local code!)

```

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Requirement Details Telepresence Connected to the cluster (telepresence connect) A deployed service e.g., backend deployment with a ClusterIP service Local dev environment Your service code running locally

docs.title: Step 1: Deploy a Sample Backend Service
docs.text:
Create a simple backend deployment and service in your cluster:

backend-deploy.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: backend
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: backend
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: backend
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: backend
        image: nginx:latest
        ports:
        - containerPort: 80
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: backend
spec:
  selector:
    app: backend
  ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 8080
      targetPort: 80
  type: ClusterIP

Apply it:

kubectl apply -f backend-deploy.yaml

Verify:

kubectl get deploy,svc backend

docs.title: Step 2: Create a Local Application
docs.text:
Create a simple local Python server that will replace the cluster service:

app.py
from http.server import HTTPServer, BaseHTTPRequestHandler
import json

class Handler(BaseHTTPRequestHandler):
    def do_GET(self):
        response = {
            "message": "Hello from LOCAL development! 🚀",
            "source": "local-machine",
            "path": self.path
        }
        self.send_response(200)
        self.send_header("Content-Type", "application/json")
        self.end_headers()
        self.wfile.write(json.dumps(response, indent=2).encode())

if __name__ == "__main__":
    server = HTTPServer(("0.0.0.0", 8080), Handler)
    print("Local server running on port 8080...")
    server.serve_forever()

Start the local server:

python3 app.py

docs.title: Step 3: Create the Global Intercept
docs.text:
In a separate terminal, run the intercept:

telepresence intercept backend --port 8080:8080
Expected Output 
✔ Intercepted
   Using Deployment backend
      Intercept name    : backend
      State             : ACTIVE
      Workload kind     : Deployment
      Intercepting      : all TCP connections
          8080 -> 8080 TCP

Note

The --port 8080:8080 flag means: forward cluster port 8080 to local port 8080.

docs.title: Task 1: Verify the Intercept is Active
docs.text:
telepresence list
Expected Output 
backend: intercepted
    Intercept name  : backend
    State           : ACTIVE
    Workload kind   : Deployment
    Intercepting    : all TCP connections

docs.title: Task 2: Test the Original Response
docs.text:
From your local machine, curl the service:

curl http://backend.default:8080
Expected Output 
{
  "message": "Hello from LOCAL development! 🚀",
  "source": "local-machine",
  "path": "/"
}

Key Insight

The response is coming from your local Python server, not from the nginx container in the cluster!

docs.title: Task 3: Modify the Code and See Instant Changes
docs.text:
Edit app.py — change the message:

response = {
    "message": "Updated response - no Docker build needed! ✨",
    "source": "local-machine",
    "path": self.path
}

Restart your local server and curl again:

curl http://backend.default:8080

Expected Result

You see the updated message immediately — without building a Docker image, pushing to a registry, or running kubectl apply.

docs.title: Task 4: Observe from the Frontend Perspective
docs.text: If you have a frontend application that calls the backend service, simply refresh the browser. The frontend will receive responses from your local code.

docs.title: Cleanup
docs.text:
Leave the intercept:

telepresence leave backend

Remove the test deployment (optional):

kubectl delete -f backend-deploy.yaml

docs.title: Outcome
docs.text:
What You Learned

  • A global intercept routes all traffic from a service to your local machine
  • You can modify local code and see changes instantly in the cluster
  • No Docker build, image push, or kubectl apply is needed during development
  • The inner dev loop is reduced from minutes to seconds
  • You have successfully bypassed the CI/CD pipeline for local iteration

docs.title: Personal Intercepts
docs.text:
Objective

Develop in a shared environment without affecting other users or teammates. Only requests with your custom header hit your local machine.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
A Personal Intercept (also called a selective or header-based intercept) only routes traffic that matches a specific HTTP header to your local machine. All other traffic continues to the original pod in the cluster. This allows multiple developers to work on the same service in a shared staging/dev environment simultaneously.

```mermaid flowchart TD A[Incoming Request] –> B{Has headerx-dev-user=yourname?} B –>|Yes| C[Your Local Machine] B –>|No| D[Original Pod in Cluster]

style C fill:#4CAF50,color:#fff
style D fill:#2196F3,color:#fff

```

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Requirement Details Telepresence Connected to the cluster A deployed service e.g., backend deployment with a ClusterIP service Local dev environment Your service code running locally on port 8080

Smart Agent Requirement

Personal intercepts require the Telepresence Traffic Agent to be injected into the target pod. This happens automatically when you create a personal intercept. Some older versions or configurations may require an API Gateway (like Emissary-Ingress).

docs.title: Step 1: Ensure the Backend Service is Running
docs.text:
Verify the backend deployment and service exist:

kubectl get deploy,svc backend

If not, deploy them using the manifest from Lab 2.

docs.title: Step 2: Start Your Local Server
docs.text:
Use the same app.py from Lab 2, or create a version with your name:

app.py
from http.server import HTTPServer, BaseHTTPRequestHandler
import json

class Handler(BaseHTTPRequestHandler):
    def do_GET(self):
        response = {
            "message": "Hello from GHOST developer! 👻",
            "developer": "yourname",
            "source": "local-machine",
            "path": self.path
        }
        self.send_response(200)
        self.send_header("Content-Type", "application/json")
        self.end_headers()
        self.wfile.write(json.dumps(response, indent=2).encode())

if __name__ == "__main__":
    server = HTTPServer(("0.0.0.0", 8080), Handler)
    print("Ghost dev server running on port 8080...")
    server.serve_forever()

Start it:

python3 app.py

docs.title: Step 3: Create a Personal Intercept with a Header
docs.text:
In a separate terminal, create the intercept with a custom header filter:

telepresence intercept backend \
  --port 8080:8080 \
  --http-header="x-dev-user=yourname"
Expected Output 
✔ Intercepted
   Using Deployment backend
      Intercept name    : backend
      State             : ACTIVE
      Workload kind     : Deployment
      Intercepting      : HTTP requests with header
          x-dev-user: yourname
          8080 -> 8080 TCP

docs.title: Task 1: Send a Standard Request (No Header)
docs.text:
curl http://backend.default:8080

Expected Result

You should see the original nginx response from the cluster pod — your local server is NOT hit.

docs.title: Task 2: Send a Request with Your Custom Header
docs.text:
curl -H "x-dev-user: yourname" http://backend.default:8080
Expected Output 
{
  "message": "Hello from GHOST developer! 👻",
  "developer": "yourname",
  "source": "local-machine",
  "path": "/"
}

Key Insight

Only the request with the x-dev-user: yourname header reaches your local machine. All other traffic goes to the original pod.

docs.title: Task 3: Simulate Multiple Developers
docs.text:
Imagine your teammate creates their own intercept:

# Teammate's terminal
telepresence intercept backend \
  --port 9090:8080 \
  --http-header="x-dev-user=teammate"

Now three scenarios exist:

Request Destination curl http://backend.default:8080 Original cluster pod curl -H "x-dev-user: yourname" http://backend.default:8080 Your local machine curl -H "x-dev-user: teammate" http://backend.default:8080 Teammate’s local machine

docs.title: Task 4: Verify with a Browser Extension
docs.text:
Browser Testing

Use a browser extension like ModHeader (Chrome) or Header Editor (Firefox) to add the x-dev-user: yourname header to all browser requests. Then access the service URL in your browser to see your local code in action.

docs.title: Cleanup
docs.text:
Leave the intercept:

telepresence leave backend

docs.title: Outcome
docs.text:
What You Learned

  • Personal intercepts route only matching requests to your local machine
  • Other users and traffic are unaffected — they hit the original pod
  • Multiple developers can intercept the same service simultaneously
  • Custom HTTP headers act as a routing key for developer-specific traffic
  • You understand how to safely “debug in production” (or staging) without causing downtime

docs.title: Env Vars & Secrets
docs.text:
Objective

Run your local code with the exact same configuration as the remote Pod — environment variables, secrets, and ConfigMap values included.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
One of the most common causes of “it works on my machine” issues is missing or different configuration. Remote pods have environment variables injected from ConfigMaps, Secrets, and the Kubernetes downward API. Telepresence can capture all of these and export them to a local .env file so your local application runs with identical configuration.

```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Cluster CM[ConfigMap] –> Pod S[Secret] –> Pod DA[Downward API] –> Pod end

Pod -->|telepresence intercept<br/>--env-file| EF[k8s-spec.env]
EF --> IDE[Your IDE / Local App]

style EF fill:#FF9800,color:#fff
style IDE fill:#4CAF50,color:#fff

```

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Requirement Details Telepresence Connected to the cluster A deployed service With ConfigMaps and/or Secrets attached Local dev environment Your IDE or terminal

docs.title: Step 1: Deploy a Backend with ConfigMaps and Secrets
docs.text:
Create a deployment that uses environment variables from ConfigMaps and Secrets:

backend-with-config.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: backend-config
data:
  DATABASE_HOST: "postgres.default.svc.cluster.local"
  DATABASE_PORT: "5432"
  DATABASE_NAME: "myapp"
  LOG_LEVEL: "debug"
  CACHE_TTL: "300"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: backend-secret
type: Opaque
stringData:
  DATABASE_USER: "admin"
  DATABASE_PASSWORD: "s3cur3-p@ssw0rd"
  API_KEY: "ak_live_abc123xyz789"
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: backend
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: backend
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: backend
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: backend
        image: nginx:latest
        ports:
        - containerPort: 80
        envFrom:
        - configMapRef:
            name: backend-config
        - secretRef:
            name: backend-secret
        env:
        - name: POD_NAME
          valueFrom:
            fieldRef:
              fieldPath: metadata.name
        - name: POD_NAMESPACE
          valueFrom:
            fieldRef:
              fieldPath: metadata.namespace
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: backend
spec:
  selector:
    app: backend
  ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 8080
      targetPort: 80
  type: ClusterIP

Apply it:

kubectl apply -f backend-with-config.yaml

Verify:

kubectl get deploy,configmap,secret backend backend-config backend-secret

docs.title: Step 2: Intercept with --env-file
docs.text:
Create an intercept and export the pod’s environment variables to a local file:

telepresence intercept backend \
  --port 8080:8080 \
  --env-file ./k8s-spec.env
Expected Output 
✔ Intercepted
   Using Deployment backend
      Intercept name    : backend
      State             : ACTIVE
      Workload kind     : Deployment
      Intercepting      : all TCP connections
          8080 -> 8080 TCP
      Environment file  : ./k8s-spec.env

docs.title: Task 1: Inspect the Generated Environment File
docs.text:
cat ./k8s-spec.env
Expected Output 
API_KEY=ak_live_abc123xyz789
CACHE_TTL=300
DATABASE_HOST=postgres.default.svc.cluster.local
DATABASE_NAME=myapp
DATABASE_PASSWORD=s3cur3-p@ssw0rd
DATABASE_PORT=5432
DATABASE_USER=admin
LOG_LEVEL=debug
POD_NAME=backend-xyz-abc
POD_NAMESPACE=default
KUBERNETES_SERVICE_HOST=10.43.0.1
KUBERNETES_SERVICE_PORT=443
...

Security Note

The .env file contains secrets in plaintext. Add it to your .gitignore immediately: 

echo "k8s-spec.env" >> .gitignore

docs.title: Task 2: Use the Env File in Your Local Application
docs.text:
Load the environment variables when running your local server:

Shell (source)Python (dotenv)Docker 
set -a && source ./k8s-spec.env && set +a
python3 app.py

from dotenv import load_dotenv
load_dotenv("./k8s-spec.env")

import os
db_host = os.getenv("DATABASE_HOST")
print(f"Connecting to {db_host}...")

docker run --env-file ./k8s-spec.env my-app

docs.title: VS Code
docs.text:
Add a launch configuration in .vscode/launch.json:

.vscode/launch.json
{
  "version": "0.2.0",
  "configurations": [
    {
      "name": "Debug with K8s Env",
      "type": "python",
      "request": "launch",
      "program": "${workspaceFolder}/app.py",
      "envFile": "${workspaceFolder}/k8s-spec.env"
    }
  ]
}

Now when you press F5, your application starts with the exact same environment as the remote pod.

docs.title: Task 4: Verify the Configuration
docs.text:
Create a test script to verify all expected variables are loaded:

verify_env.py
import os

required_vars = [
    "DATABASE_HOST",
    "DATABASE_PORT",
    "DATABASE_NAME",
    "DATABASE_USER",
    "DATABASE_PASSWORD",
    "API_KEY",
    "LOG_LEVEL",
]

print("Environment Variable Check:")
print("-" * 40)
for var in required_vars:
    value = os.getenv(var, "❌ NOT SET")
    # Mask sensitive values
    if "PASSWORD" in var or "KEY" in var:
        display = value[:3] + "***" if value != "❌ NOT SET" else value
    else:
        display = value
    print(f"  {var}: {display}")

Run it:

set -a && source ./k8s-spec.env && set +a
python3 verify_env.py

docs.title: Cleanup
docs.text:
Leave the intercept:

telepresence leave backend

Remove the generated env file:

rm ./k8s-spec.env

docs.title: Outcome
docs.text:
What You Learned

  • Telepresence can export a pod’s full environment to a local .env file
  • ConfigMap values, Secrets, and Downward API fields are all captured
  • Your IDE/debugger can load the .env file for identical runtime configuration
  • You eliminate “it works on my machine” issues caused by missing configuration
  • Always add .env files to .gitignore to prevent leaking secrets

docs.title: Volume Mounts & Debugging
docs.text:
Objective

Handle file-based dependencies (like SSL certs or ConfigMaps) and perform live debugging of cluster services from your IDE.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Some services depend on files mounted into their pods — ConfigMaps, Secrets as files, SSL certificates, or custom configuration files. Telepresence can mount these remote volumes locally using sshfs, so your local code can read the same files the pod uses. Combined with an IDE debugger, this gives you the ultimate local development experience.

```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph Cluster Pod V1[“/etc/config/settings.json(ConfigMap)”] V2[“/etc/ssl/certs/tls.crt(Secret)”] V3[“/var/data/cache(PVC)”] end

subgraph Local Machine
    TP["$TELEPRESENCE_ROOT"]
    TP --> LV1["$TELEPRESENCE_ROOT/etc/config/settings.json"]
    TP --> LV2["$TELEPRESENCE_ROOT/etc/ssl/certs/tls.crt"]
    TP --> LV3["$TELEPRESENCE_ROOT/var/data/cache"]
    LV1 --> IDE[IDE + Debugger]
    LV2 --> IDE
    LV3 --> IDE
end

V1 -.->|sshfs mount| LV1
V2 -.->|sshfs mount| LV2
V3 -.->|sshfs mount| LV3

style TP fill:#FF9800,color:#fff
style IDE fill:#4CAF50,color:#fff

```

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Requirement Details Telepresence Connected to the cluster sshfs Installed on your local machine A deployed service With ConfigMap/Secret volume mounts IDE with debugger VS Code, GoLand, PyCharm, etc.

docs.title: Install sshfs
docs.text:
macOSLinux 
brew install macfuse
brew install gromgit/fuse/sshfs-mac

Warning

macFUSE requires a system extension. You may need to allow it in System Preferences > Privacy & Security and reboot.

sudo apt-get install sshfs

docs.title: Step 1: Deploy a Service with Volume Mounts
docs.text:
Create a deployment with a ConfigMap mounted as a file:

backend-with-volumes.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: app-settings
data:
  settings.json: |
    {
      "app_name": "Telepresence Lab",
      "version": "1.0.0",
      "features": {
        "dark_mode": true,
        "beta_features": false,
        "max_connections": 100
      },
      "endpoints": {
        "auth": "http://auth.default.svc.cluster.local",
        "cache": "http://redis.default.svc.cluster.local:6379"
      }
    }
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: tls-certs
type: Opaque
stringData:
  tls.crt: |
    -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
    (sample certificate data)
    -----END CERTIFICATE-----
  tls.key: |
    -----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY-----
    (sample key data)
    -----END PRIVATE KEY-----
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: backend
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: backend
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: backend
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: backend
        image: nginx:latest
        ports:
        - containerPort: 80
        volumeMounts:
        - name: config
          mountPath: /etc/config
          readOnly: true
        - name: certs
          mountPath: /etc/ssl/app-certs
          readOnly: true
      volumes:
      - name: config
        configMap:
          name: app-settings
      - name: certs
        secret:
          secretName: tls-certs
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: backend
spec:
  selector:
    app: backend
  ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 8080
      targetPort: 80
  type: ClusterIP

Apply it:

kubectl apply -f backend-with-volumes.yaml

Verify the volumes are mounted:

kubectl exec deploy/backend -- ls /etc/config/
kubectl exec deploy/backend -- cat /etc/config/settings.json

docs.title: Step 2: Intercept with Volume Mounts
docs.text:
Create an intercept with the --mount flag:

telepresence intercept backend \
  --port 8080:8080 \
  --mount=true
Expected Output 
✔ Intercepted
   Using Deployment backend
      Intercept name    : backend
      State             : ACTIVE
      Workload kind     : Deployment
      Intercepting      : all TCP connections
          8080 -> 8080 TCP
      Volume Mount Point: /tmp/telfs-xxxxxxx

TELEPRESENCE_ROOT

Telepresence sets the $TELEPRESENCE_ROOT environment variable pointing to the mount directory. All remote filesystem paths are available under this prefix.

docs.title: Task 1: Explore the Mounted Volumes
docs.text:
List the mounted files:

echo $TELEPRESENCE_ROOT
ls $TELEPRESENCE_ROOT/etc/config/
cat $TELEPRESENCE_ROOT/etc/config/settings.json
Expected Output 
{
  "app_name": "Telepresence Lab",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "features": {
    "dark_mode": true,
    "beta_features": false,
    "max_connections": 100
  },
  "endpoints": {
    "auth": "http://auth.default.svc.cluster.local",
    "cache": "http://redis.default.svc.cluster.local:6379"
  }
}

Also check the mounted certificates:

ls $TELEPRESENCE_ROOT/etc/ssl/app-certs/

docs.title: Task 2: Use Mounted Files in Your Local Code
docs.text:
Create an application that reads the remote ConfigMap:

app_with_config.py
import json
import os
from http.server import HTTPServer, BaseHTTPRequestHandler

# Read config from the mounted volume
telepresence_root = os.getenv("TELEPRESENCE_ROOT", "")
config_path = os.path.join(telepresence_root, "etc/config/settings.json")

with open(config_path) as f:
    config = json.load(f)

print(f"Loaded config: {config['app_name']} v{config['version']}")

class Handler(BaseHTTPRequestHandler):
    def do_GET(self):
        response = {
            "app": config["app_name"],
            "version": config["version"],
            "features": config["features"],
            "source": "local-with-remote-config"
        }
        self.send_response(200)
        self.send_header("Content-Type", "application/json")
        self.end_headers()
        self.wfile.write(json.dumps(response, indent=2).encode())

if __name__ == "__main__":
    server = HTTPServer(("0.0.0.0", 8080), Handler)
    print("Server running on port 8080 with remote config...")
    server.serve_forever()

Run it:

python3 app_with_config.py

Test it:

curl http://backend.default:8080

docs.title: Task 3: Attach a Debugger (VS Code)
docs.text:
Create a VS Code debug configuration:

.vscode/launch.json
{
  "version": "0.2.0",
  "configurations": [
    {
      "name": "Debug with Telepresence",
      "type": "python",
      "request": "launch",
      "program": "${workspaceFolder}/app_with_config.py",
      "envFile": "${workspaceFolder}/k8s-spec.env",
      "env": {
        "TELEPRESENCE_ROOT": "${env:TELEPRESENCE_ROOT}"
      }
    }
  ]
}

Steps:

  1. Set a breakpoint in app_with_config.py on the do_GET method.
  2. Press F5 to start debugging.

  3. From another terminal, trigger a request:

    curl http://backend.default:8080

  4. Watch the execution pause at your breakpoint in VS Code.

Key Insight

You are debugging a cluster service as if it were a local application, with full access to remote config files and the cluster network.

docs.title: Task 4: Inspect Remote Filesystem Structure
docs.text:
Explore the full remote filesystem available to your pod:

# See what's available
find $TELEPRESENCE_ROOT -maxdepth 3 -type f | head -30

This gives you visibility into:

  • Mounted ConfigMaps and Secrets
  • Service account tokens
  • Kubernetes downward API files
  • Any PersistentVolumeClaim data

docs.title: Cleanup
docs.text:
Leave the intercept:

telepresence leave backend

Remove test resources:

kubectl delete -f backend-with-volumes.yaml

Disconnect from the cluster:

telepresence quit

docs.title: Outcome
docs.text:
What You Learned

  • Telepresence can mount remote pod volumes locally via sshfs
  • ConfigMaps, Secrets, and PVCs are accessible under $TELEPRESENCE_ROOT
  • Your local code can read the same files as the remote pod
  • You can attach an IDE debugger and set breakpoints on intercepted traffic
  • You have mastered debugging complex, stateful microservices as if they were monolithic local apps
